original_abstract: Neural machine translation (NMT) is a burgeoning approach to statistical translation that relies entirely on deep neural networks. Typically, these models utilize an encoder-decoder architecture: an encoder compresses a variable-length source sentence into a fixed-length vector, which a decoder then expands into a target translation. In this paper, we investigate the performance and limitations of NMT by comparing two specific architectures: the standard RNN Encoder-Decoder and a newly developed gated recursive convolutional neural network (grConv). Our analysis reveals that while NMT models perform competitively on short sentences with familiar vocabulary, their accuracy drops sharply as sentence length increases or when they encounter unknown words. Additionally, we demonstrate that the proposed grConv model is capable of automatically discovering and capturing the grammatical structure of sentences without explicit linguistic supervision.
humanized_abstract: We investigate the strengths and limitations of neural machine translation (NMT) approaches to statistical translation and examine the performance of two types of neural translation models: (1) the state-of-the-art RNN Encoder-Decoder, and (2) a novel gated recursive convolutional neural network (grConv) approach. We compare their capabilities in translating various types of input sentences, with a focus on exploring the effects of increasing sentence length on the accuracy of the NMT models. Experimental results show that, although NMT models are highly proficient in translating short sentences and for well-known vocabularies, their performance deteriorates drastically as the input length of a sentence increases, and especially when encountering the lack of knowledge of some uncommon words. Experiments also show that our proposed grConv model can learn and extract meaningful grammatical structures from input data in an unsupervised manner.

Experimental results show that, although NMT models are highly proficient in translating short sentences and for well-known vocabularies, their performance deteriorates drastically as the input length of a sentence increases, and especially when encountering the lack of knowledge of some uncommon words. Experiments also show that our proposed grConv model can learn and extract meaningful grammatical structures from input data in an unsupervised manner.
